macro_segments.title: The Debate between Rabbi Yehuda and Rabbi Meir on Splitting the Live and Dead Animal

micro_segments.title: Introduction to the Mishnah dispute on how to interpret the biblical verses about splitting the live and dead animal
Rabbi Meir's interpretation: the dead animal is worth nothing, and only the depreciation due to death is split
Rabbi Yehuda's interpretation: literally split both the live and dead animal, making the mazik a partial owner of the carcass
The condition that makes Rabbi Yehuda's reading work: the animals must be of equal value
Rabbi Yehuda's position clarified: the mazik is a partial owner of the carcass only to benefit from appreciation, not to suffer from depreciation

macro_segments.title: Rabbi Yochanan's Resolution of the Debate: The Difference Lies in Appreciation of the Carcass

micro_segments.title: Rabbi Yochanan introduces the principle that the dispute concerns only cases where the carcass increases in value
In depreciation cases, both agree the nizak (injured party) suffers the loss
In appreciation cases, Rabbi Meir holds the nizak retains all appreciation, while Rabbi Yehuda holds the mazik gains half of the appreciation

macro_segments.title: Rabbi Yehuda's Logical Problem: The Scenario of Unequal-Valued Animals

micro_segments.title: Rabbi Yehuda raises the problem: if the Torah shows compassion on the mazik by letting him gain from carcass appreciation, why can't this apply to unequal-valued animals?
The case: a 20-shekel ox gores a 100-shekel ox; the carcass depreciates to 50 shekels
If split literally per the verses: mazik takes 35 shekels (10 from live, 25 from dead) and walks away with a profit from an ox originally worth 20
The absurdity: the mazik would gain rather than pay damages, contradicting the principle that the damager should not profit
The verse states the damager pays and does not take, excluding the possibility of profit

macro_segments.title: Resolution: The Verses Apply Only When Animals Are of Equal Value

micro_segments.title: The gemara clarifies that the prohibition on mazik profit applies when the nizak would clearly lose money
The case where both animals start at 20 shekels but the carcass appreciates to 30 shekels: both parties gain, though the nizak gains less than if the animal had not been gored
Even in the mutual gain case, the Torah does not permit the mazik to own the carcass outright, only to offset his payment obligation through appreciation
The fundamental agreement: all parties acknowledge that when animals differ in value, the mazik pays only half the depreciation from live to dead, not a literal split of both animals

macro_segments.title: Cases Where One Party Is Liable for Payment and the Other Is Exempt

micro_segments.title: Introduction to the Mishnah distinguishing when an ox's action creates liability but a human's action creates exemption, and vice versa
Case 1: If an ox causes embarrassment, the owner is exempt; if a human causes embarrassment, they are liable
Case 2: If an ox knocks out the eye or tooth of a slave, the owner is exempt; if a human does so, the slave goes free
Case 3: If an ox injures the owner's parent, the owner is liable; if the owner injures the parent, the owner is exempt because they are liable for execution
Case 4: If an ox sets fire to a haystack on Shabbat, the owner is liable; if a human does so, the owner is exempt because they violated Shabbat, a capital crime

macro_segments.title: The Principle of Kimlei: Capital Crimes Override Monetary Liability

micro_segments.title: Rabbi Abbahu's teaching: all destructive acts on Shabbat incur no liability, except wounding and fire, which do incur liability
Rabbi Yochanan rejects Abbahu's teaching and insists that even wounding and fire incur no liability unless there is constructive intent
Debate: whether constructive intent (like needing the ashes) is required, or whether the physical act of wounding/fire suffices to define Shabbat violation
Example of a clever ox with a gum infection uncovering a barrel of mead to soothe its gums, illustrating that animals can have constructive intent
Rava's resolution: the Mishnah refers to unintentional (shogeg) violation of Shabbat, and kimlei applies even without intentional violation
Chizkiya's teaching: there is a distinction between acts deserving of capital punishment and acts deserving of monetary payment; capital crimes override all monetary liability
The principle of kimlei when a capital crime is involved: even unintentional commission of a capital crime exempts from monetary liability
Rashi vs. Tosafot debate: whether constructive intent is required to define an act as a capital crime for kimlei purposes

macro_segments.title: The Burden of Proof Principle: Hamotzi Mechavero Alav Haraya

micro_segments.title: Introduction to the Mishnah cases involving ambiguity about which ox caused damage and who bears the burden of proof
Case 1: One ox runs after another; it is unclear if goring caused the injury or if the ox tripped on a rock; the claimant must prove the goring
Case 2: Two oxes pursue one; it is unclear which one gored; both are exempt because neither can be proven liable
Case 3: Both oxes belong to one owner; both are liable because the claimant can prove one of the owner's oxes caused damage, even if the specific ox is unknown
Case 4: One large and one small ox; the claimant says the large one gored (to maximize recovery), defendant says the small one; burden of proof on claimant
Case 5: One ox is tam (innocent) and one is muad (forewarned); claimant says the muad gored (for full recovery), defendant says the tam; burden of proof on claimant
Cases 6-7: Multiple damages and multiple damagers in various combinations, all governed by hamotzi mechavero alav haraya

macro_segments.title: Gemara Debate: Does the Mishnah Allow Collection on the Defendant's Admitted Claim?

micro_segments.title: The Mishnah is understood as teaching against Sumchos, who holds that monetary doubts are split between parties
Sumchos would split even in cases of definite claims (bari v'bari) where both parties insist their version is true
Challenge: the Mishnah describes both parties making definite claims, so how does the claimant recover anything if he cannot prove his claim?
Rav Pappa's question: can the claimant at least recover based on the defendant's admitted lesser claim (like the wheat/barley case)?
Comparison case: I demand wheat, you admit only barley; I receive nothing because my definite claim for wheat implicitly rejects barley
The question whether the Mishnah involves definite claims on both sides (bari v'bari) or definite claim versus uncertainty (bari v'shema)

topical_tags.term: Hamotzi mechavero alav haraya (burden of proof)
